Correct me if I am wrong. We originally support customized key generating method (reducer and splitter), but the values cannot be changed. Therefore, you are proposing this hook for changing the values.

I think this is not related to the flatten or unflatten process, but might be good to have for saving memory usage or computing time. We can do all the things we want in one dict creation process instead of having some intermediate dict and doing some post processing. If this is the purpose, and think there are some other ways that are more flexible and pythonic.

For example, we can make a generator `Flatten.items()`:
```
{key: hook(key, value) for key, value in Flatten(d).items()}
```
I think this will make the code much more readable. I'm actually planning to make a Flatten class like this, so please tell me your suggestion.

@jofsky I think we should take a different approach on this for a few reasons:
1. The release notes framework uses a concept of configurable "parsers". A project can override project__git__release_notes__parsers to change which sections are rendered, and in what order.  The release info should be handled by a parser so that it's consistent with how other sections of the release notes are handled.
2. There is already a parser called InstallLinkParser which adds the install links if a version id was set as a task option (like it is when the task is called as part of the release flows). We should use or modify this parser rather than creating a new way of doing the same thing.
3. You've hardcoded the assumptions that the Sandbox push will happen on the same day the release is made and the Production push will happen 6 days later. This is usually the case for Salesforce.org, but may not be valid for other users of CumulusCI or if we change our release cadence in the future. I think we discussed this a while back and I suggested that the task should take options for specifying both the sandbox date and production date.

I'm pretty sure that `impl Display for Key` _should_ actually display the Python `repr` for the `Key`.

If there are particular codepaths (such as the one for `Snapshot`, or other workunit rendering code) that don't want to display the "entire" underlying object, I think that they should use the `EngineAware` trait to conditionally render `Key`'s/`Value`s based on their types.

A middle ground would be to have `impl Display for Key` use the `EngineAware` trait under the hood... I'm less sure that that is a good behavior though, because it feels like the `EngineAware` trait should be specific to workunit creation.

I noticed that LLVM/clang can emit multiple sections with the same name in order to save space in the resulting binary when -ffunction-sections is used. The current code also doesn't handle this case, so using get_section_by_name() on such an object file will always return the last section with the non-unique name. See the following example:

```
$ cat two_f.c
void a(){}
void b(){}

$ clang-9 -ffunction-sections -fno-unique-section-names -c -o two_f.o two_f.c

$ readelf -S two_f.o
There are 11 section headers, starting at offset 0x208:

Section Headers:
  [Nr] Name              Type             Address           Offset
       Size              EntSize          Flags  Link  Info  Align
  [ 0]                   NULL             0000000000000000  00000000
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000           0     0     0
  [ 1] .strtab           STRTAB           0000000000000000  000001b0
       0000000000000057  0000000000000000           0     0     1
  [ 2] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000000  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     4
  [ 3] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000040
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
  [ 4] .text             PROGBITS         0000000000000000  00000050
       0000000000000006  0000000000000000  AX       0     0     16
[...]

$ python3
Python 3.6.9 (default, Jul 17 2020, 12:50:27)
[GCC 8.4.0] on linux
>>> from elftools.elf.elffile import ELFFile
>>> elf = ELFFile(open('two_f.o', 'rb'))
>>> print(elf.get_section_by_name('.text').header)
Container({'sh_name': 1, 'sh_type': 'SHT_PROGBITS', 'sh_flags': 6, 'sh_addr': 0, 'sh_offset': 80, 'sh_size': 6, 'sh_link': 0, 'sh_info': 0, 'sh_addralign': 16, 'sh_entsize': 0})
```

In the same manner, your code will always return the last index (in our example above 4) for '.text'.

I'm not sure if that's something that needs general fixing but I would like to keep that in mind.

I haven't forgotten about this PR, but I'm not ready to review it yet. There are many places in the R package which deal with references to numeration (i.e. where users have to provide a number to refer to "the `n`th thing"), and those references use a mix of 1-based and 0-based indexing.

Before considering this proposal to control that behavior with a boolean flag in one limited place, I'd like to list out all such places in the package's public API and see if we can come up with a general standard for when the R package uses 1-based numeration and when it uses 0-based numeration.

I'll try to complete that list and open an a "request for comment (RFC)" issue with a proposal soon.

Hi @maxhgerlach, this PR is looking excellent! I have done a first brief pass over the code and left a few initial comments. 

I think this is very well designed, which shows given the relatively minimal changes required to the op implementations once you set up the process sets.

I'm kind of questioning whether the dynamic addition/subtraction of process sets is worth the added complexity of this PR. Trying to think of real use cases that would require this (other than providing a more convenient API for lazier users :) ).  The static initialization is nice since it better enforces the requirement that all process sets are defined on all processes, in the same order. However, you've already implemented it, so there is that too.

Speaking of yarn invocations, [apparently](https://yarnpkg.com/features/zero-installs) with Yarn 2+ it's... well, _debatably_ a good idea, but at the very least "not wrong" (according to **them**, anyway) to check into the repo either:

1. The Yarn version in use, so that everyone is running the same tools, or
2. The _entire_ dependency cache, iff it's in the form of compressed package archives rather than a giant, exploded `node_modules` hierarchy. That requires switching to using [their `pnp` linker](https://yarnpkg.com/features/pnp), instead of the `node-modules` one that's the default for projects coming from yarn 1.

Not necessities by any stretch, but apparently options possibly worth considering.

Got a general suggestion on how you could tweak this functionality slightly, so I'll save any small review points for now...

As I understand it this is piggybacking on the existing language version functionality, so using `owner` and differentiating the versions by number and then getting the latest version for a language (if enabled) in the middleware.

However, I think there's an important distinction between the language versions and "content" versions of pages. We only know what language a user wants at the point a request is made, so we have to look for the correct version in the middleware. With content versions we already know which version we should show to the user before the request is made because we don't care about old versions (except for staff members).

There might be another way of handling it but here is my suggestion:
Add another foreign key relation to `page` on the `Page` model and call it something like `version_for`, keep `version` for ordering purposes and `verbose_name` `owner` to something more language specific. Then in the `PageManager` by default we can filter out any pages that have `version_for` set and we can either add another middleware class `VersioningMiddleware` or extend `PublicationMiddleware` to serve up different versions on the frontend for admins.

Pass the scopes to the protocol `get_scopes_for` method and then pass the returned scopes to the `Connection` (or directly to the method `get_authorization_url`if you prefer).

I think I follow the method from reading the code. You basically compute an equation that is the integral of the interpolant over an image. It's linear so you solve it using a direct solver. 

The tests look complete. I cannot comment on the other random bug fixes. 

I do have one performance suggestion. If I understand the method correctly, for a fixed image size and interpolant type, the matrix A that you run through the LLT decomposition is independent of the underlying image. I am betting most of the compute time is this decomposition and not the final solve step (called back substitution IIRC). I am guessing most folks dealing with psf models will draw with the same size and interpolant always (or would be willing to anyways). Thus if we cache the matrix decomposition, we should be able to have a fair more efficiency in the code. I also could be misunderstanding things. 

Final note is that I did not check the unit integrals of the interpolants in detail. However the tests look like they cover any possible mistakes in the analytic ones.

@Charlottez112 thanks for the changes. The code looks pretty good, but I think that there are some cases that are probably not covered at the moment (for example, what happens if both `out` and `dtype` are provided to `_convert_array` and they don't match?). I think what would help a lot with this PR is if you could make a list of possible code paths so that we can really identify all the ways this might get called and what the expected behavior should be in this case. For example, something like:
> box.wrap called with vecs of dtype np.float and out=None: input array must be copied since `box.wrap` shouldn't happen in place, the copied array must have the right shape
Don't worry about what numpy functions exist for things etc, first figure out how things _should_ work. Start by just focusing on how `box.wrap` should behave, then once we iron out those details we can figure out what `_convert_array` should handle in each of these cases.

Regarding copying, we can figure out how to minimize copies, and decide whether there are cases where we can guarantee that no copies will be made (even internally) and whether there should be a way to trigger errors if a copy is necessary.

@peelstnac great work. Here are a couple of overall comments for you to work on:

1. The error should _not_ trigger if there is already a type annotation for the parameter/attribute. So for example, `x: type = int` shouldn't trigger the error, but also `x: int = int` shouldn't either, even though the default value doesn't match the type annotation (this is checked by a different checker).
2. Right now the check is too specialized; it doesn't handle subscript expressions like `x = list[int]`, or types from somewhere other than `builtins`. Instead of checking the literal name, try calling `.inferred()` on the node; you'll get a list of possible values that the node evaluates to. Access the first item in the list and check whether it's a `type` or not. (If the list is empty, don't trigger the error---this means the inference failed, and so we should be conservative and not report errors in this case.)
3. Make sure to add additional tests for all of the functionality described above.
4. Add the checker to the HTML documentation, and to the Changelog (since it's a user-facing change).

Thanks for implementing this, @DaelonSuzuka !

I didn't think about it before, but we need to set `__cause__` on the error, so we can:

* Actually indicate that that it was an attribute access that was the proximate cause of the error, and inform the user which attribute required PyOpenGL
* Give the details of the ImportError/ModuleNotFound error that happened on import, giving them useful information about what actually happened rather than leaving them (and us or whomever they report it to) guessing whether the module is missing, there's a DLL issue, it was installed/uninstalled incorrectly, it was pip/conda mixing, etc.

Therefore, after a bunch of experimentation, since this added a lot of logic to every call site, I ended up putting this in a helper utility function and and calling that in `__getattr__`.

I was originally going to make this as suggestions, but it turned out to be rather complicated, and prevent putting the common logic into a utility function somewhere else. Thus, I ended up just pushing it to [a branch on my fork](https://github.com/CAM-Gerlach/qtpy/tree/improve-import-hygiene) you can pull with

```bash
git remote add cam-gerlach https://github.com/CAM-Gerlach/qtpy.git
git fetch cam-gerlach
git pull cam-gerlach improve-import-hygiene
```

Or, I can push it to your branch directly, if you prefer. Thanks!

1. The current implementation effectively constraints the first t beam search steps to the target prefix, which is neat and probably sufficient for most use cases where the target prefix is short. Impressive work! It's surprising that this functionality could be added in such little code. Although, I'd like for us to think carefully about whether it makes sense to modify the ``ParamConeProg`` and ``Dcp2Cone`` classes or subclass them. Here are some thoughts:

* I'm not a fan of changing the ``ParamConeProg`` class so that it allows nonlinear objectives. I'd prefer to define a new class called ``ParamQuadConeProg`` that subclasses ``ParamConeProg``. If it seems like doing so would require a lot of code duplication then I think that's an argument to simplify some of the functions in the ``ParamConeProg`` class to handle smaller units of functionality a piece.

* I also think that ``Dcp2Cone`` should be clearer with its scope as to affine versus quadratic representations. I can see how defining a new canonicalization class might be overkill, but perhaps a name change is in order.
